Name: dynamic_latent_space_inference
Title: Dynamic Latent Space Inference: Training-Free Real-Time Adaptation for Latency-Aware Machine Learning
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamic adaptations of latent space representations, guided by real-time activation diagnostics, can significantly enhance the efficiency and accuracy of latency-critical ML inference without requiring additional training.
Related Work: This proposal builds on recent training-free frameworks in ML inference (e.g., Spectral Quantization Routing and Geometric Signal Separation), but it distinguishes itself by focusing on real-time latent space manipulations to adaptively optimize inference paths based on current input dynamics, rather than relying solely on static or heuristic methodologies.
Abstract: In this work, we propose a novel training-free framework for dynamic latent space inference, aimed at optimizing ML model performance under strict latency constraints. Our approach utilizes real-time diagnostic metrics from activation covariances to dynamically modulate latent space representations during inference. By projecting activations onto low-rank subspaces that reflect the current input's characteristics, we ensure that computational resources are allocated efficiently, significantly improving throughput without compromising accuracy. We introduce a Jacobian-based mechanism to guide this manipulation, allowing for explicit layer-skipping strategies that minimize hidden-state perturbations and respect predefined latency budgets. Our empirical evaluation will demonstrate the framework's effectiveness across various ML tasks, highlighting its adaptability and robustness under diverse operational conditions. This research aims to bridge the gap between theoretical optimization landscapes and practical, scalable inference mechanisms, paving the way for more efficient deployment of machine learning systems in real-world applications.
Experiments: Evaluate the dynamic adaptation mechanism across standard ML benchmarks, comparing performance against static baseline models.
Test the Jacobian-based layer-skipping strategy under varying latency budgets and analyze its impact on accuracy and throughput.
Conduct ablation studies to quantify the effect of real-time covariance diagnostics on the efficiency of latent space adaptations.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the challenge of achieving real-time diagnostics without incurring excessive computational overhead. Additionally, the effectiveness of the proposed method may vary across different model architectures and input distributions, which requires careful evaluation and tuning.
